product.name: crible
product.problem: Serious fundamental screening across ALL of Europe (and the world) is locked behind expensive feeds (EODHD €59.99/mo, FMP Ultimate $149/mo); free tools are US-centric (Finviz), shallow, or SaaS with no control over data, formulas or universe. Investors who want transparent, reproducible fundamental screens over European small/mid caps have no self-hosted option.
product.valueProp: A screener you own: worldwide universe with Europe-depth, 100% functional with zero API keys (guaranteed forever), transparent formulas (financetoolkit + published score definitions), audited ESEF figures for EU names, DuckDB-fast filtering, and a single optional paid switch (EODHD) ready when deeper history is wanted — never required.
users: Individual fundamental investor (Maxime) screening European + worldwide equities with value/quality/forensic criteria
Technical tinkerer who self-hosts the stack, tunes the crawl priorities and writes custom screens via the DSL or SQL
metrics: Screen the full ~161k-equity worldwide universe on fundamentals in under 1 second locally (DuckDB over Parquet)
Europe as depth priority: EU companies enriched with audited ESEF XBRL figures and always crawled first
Zero-key guarantee: every core flow (ingest, compute, screen, UI) works with no API key configured, for life
Sustain a complete worldwide fundamentals sweep within Yahoo's free rate tolerance (~360 req/h, full cycle ≈ 19 days, quarterly freshness)
One-switch paid upgrade path: EODHD Fundamentals plugin specced and stubbed so paying €59.99/mo replaces the fragile yfinance link without touching the rest

inScope: Ratio and score computation into a wide snapshot
Filter DSL compiled to DuckDB SQL
outOfScope: Technical analysis, chart patterns or momentum signals
Portfolio management, backtesting or performance tracking
Multi-user SaaS: no accounts, no auth, single self-hosted operator
Buying any paid data plan in v1 (paid providers are specced as PRDs only)
assumptions: The team is: Solo developer (Maxime), TDD workflow (red-green-refactor).
The timeline is: v1 zero-key end-to-end first; phase 2 (free-key plugins) after.
The budget is: €0 for data in v1 (strict zero-key core; free-key plugins in phase 2; EODHD €59.99/mo is a documented future option, not a v1 dependency).
Compliance applies: MIT license, Respect Yahoo rate tolerance (~360 req/h budget with jitter+backoff), English codebase and docs.

functional.title: Worldwide universe built from FinanceDatabase
functional.description: Bootstrap the screening universe from FinanceDatabase (160,995 equities, 117 countries, 84 exchanges; Yahoo-suffixed symbols usable directly by yfinance [E2][E16]). Loaded into DuckDB with region tags that drive crawl priority (Europe first). market_cap arrives categorical (Large/Mid/Small) [E17]; numeric market cap is derived later from prices × shares. Known upstream data-quality caveats (CUSIP collisions [E25], stale exchange codes [E27]) are tolerated: the universe is metadata, not valuation input.

acceptance.given: a fresh install with no API keys configured
acceptance.when: the operator runs the universe bootstrap (crible ingest --bootstrap)
acceptance.then: a companies table exists in DuckDB with at least 150,000 equity rows spanning at least 100 countries, every row carrying a Yahoo-suffixed symbol, country, region, sector, industry and exchange, and re-running the bootstrap is idempotent (upsert; row count stable)

acceptance.given: the FinanceDatabase source is unreachable (offline machine, GitHub down)
acceptance.then: it exits non-zero naming FinanceDatabase as the failed source and the existing universe table is left untouched (no partial load)

acceptance.given: the universe is loaded
acceptance.then: every EU/EEA/UK/CH listing carries region = 'europe' and the highest crawl priority tier, verified by a count per region exposed in crible status

functional.title: Rolling prioritized keyless ingestion (yfinance)
functional.description: A continuous, resumable crawler fetches per-ticker fundamentals (income/balance/cash-flow, ~4 annual periods and 4–5 quarters — Yahoo's ceiling [E98][E99]) and daily prices through yfinance, under a global rate budget with jittered exponential backoff on 429 (Yahoo tolerates roughly 360 req/h and rate-limiting is Yahoo policy, not a library bug [E3][E52]). A priority queue orders work Europe → US large caps → rest of world, revisit driven by expected reporting freshness (quarterly for statements, daily for prices). Raw responses land as versioned Parquet (append-only raw layer). All sources sit behind one Provider interface; a keyed plugin with no key disables itself cleanly (zero-key contract).

acceptance.given: the universe is loaded and no API keys are configured
acceptance.when: the crawler runs for one hour
acceptance.then: it fetches statements and prices exclusively via keyless providers, issues at most 330 Yahoo requests in any rolling 60-minute window, persists each fetched company as versioned raw Parquet under the data directory, and updates its crawl queue (lastCrawledAt / nextDue) after every symbol

acceptance.given: Yahoo answers with HTTP 429 or a crumb/cookie failure
acceptance.then: it backs off exponentially with jitter (delay doubling from 1 minute up to a 15-minute cap), never busy-loops, reschedules the failed symbol instead of dropping it, and logs the event with symbol and wait time

acceptance.given: the crawler process is killed mid-cycle
acceptance.then: it resumes from the persisted queue and does not re-fetch symbols still inside their freshness window (quarterly for fundamentals, daily for prices)

acceptance.given: a European listing and a rest-of-world listing are both due for crawl
acceptance.when: the scheduler picks the next symbol
acceptance.then: the European listing is fetched first (priority order Europe → US large caps → rest of world)

functional.title: Ratio and score computation into a wide snapshot
functional.description: Compute the wide screening snapshot: financetoolkit (with the Yahoo source enforced) supplies 150+ transparently-defined ratios plus Piotroski F-Score and Altman Z-Score [E1][E6][E8]; Beneish M-Score is implemented in-house (absent from financetoolkit — verified by full-source grep) with its 8 components, unit-tested against published examples. Output: one row per company × fiscal period, ~200 columns, written as snapshot Parquet that DuckDB queries. Missing inputs yield NULL cells with provenance, never fabricated values [E30].

acceptance.given: raw statements exist for a sample of at least 100 companies
acceptance.then: a snapshot Parquet is produced with one row per company × fiscal period, at least 150 ratio columns plus piotroski_f (0–9), altman_z and beneish_m with their components, and re-running on the same input produces byte-identical output

acceptance.given: a company lacks a field required by a ratio or score (e.g. no cash-flow statement)
acceptance.then: the affected cells are NULL with a provenance note naming the missing input, the company still appears in the snapshot, and no value is imputed or fabricated

acceptance.given: the published Beneish test vectors (Beneish 1999 example set)
acceptance.then: each of the 8 components (DSRI, GMI, AQI, SGI, DEPI, SGAI, LVGI, TATA) and the final M = -4.84 + weighted sum match the published values within 0.01

functional.title: Filter DSL compiled to DuckDB SQL
functional.description: A human-readable filter DSL — e.g. roe > 15 AND piotroski >= 7 AND country IN ('FR','DE') AND sector = 'Industrials' — compiled to parametrized DuckDB SQL over the snapshot. Strict column whitelist (parser rejects unknown fields with a hint), values always bound as parameters (injection impossible by construction), sorting and pagination built in. DuckDB over Parquet is the 'ultra-powerful engine': a full 161k × 200 screen runs in milliseconds [E4][E12]; the preset-filtering timeout that hit xang1234/stock-screener under Postgres [E88] is the anti-pattern this design avoids.

acceptance.given: the snapshot is loaded
acceptance.when: a user screens with roe > 15 AND piotroski >= 7 AND country IN ('FR','DE')
acceptance.then: the result contains exactly the rows satisfying every clause, respects the requested sort and pagination, and the generated SQL references only whitelisted columns with all values bound as parameters

acceptance.given: a query names an unknown field (moat > 3) or is malformed (roe >)
acceptance.then: parsing fails with an error naming the offending token and its position plus the closest valid field name, and nothing is executed against DuckDB

acceptance.given: a hostile query string (e.g. roe > 15; DROP TABLE companies-- or a value embedding ' OR 1=1)
acceptance.then: the input is either rejected or treated strictly as a literal value — property-based tests over generated inputs prove non-whitelisted SQL never reaches DuckDB

acceptance.given: the full ~161k-row snapshot
acceptance.when: any valid DSL screen runs on a development laptop
acceptance.then: the 95th-percentile end-to-end latency is under 1 second

functional.title: CLI (crible)
functional.description: The crible CLI (Typer [E92]): crible screen "<dsl>" with table or CSV output; crible ingest (bootstrap / one cycle / continuous loop); crible compute; crible status (universe size, coverage %, freshness histogram, requests-per-hour, provider health); crible export. Exactly the same DSL and semantics as the API and UI.

acceptance.given: a working install with a computed snapshot
acceptance.then: matching rows stream to stdout as CSV with a header row, the process exits 0, and the same query through the API returns the same rows

acceptance.given: an invalid DSL string or a missing snapshot
acceptance.then: the process exits non-zero with an actionable stderr message (for a missing snapshot: telling the operator to run crible ingest / crible compute first)

acceptance.given: the crawler has been running
acceptance.then: it reports universe row count, fundamentals coverage %, a freshness histogram, the rolling requests-per-hour figure and per-provider health, in under 2 seconds

functional.title: HTTP API (FastAPI)
functional.description: The HTTP API (FastAPI [E13]): POST /screen (DSL + sort + pagination → rows, total, tookMs), GET /screen.csv (streaming export), GET /presets, GET /company/{symbol} (profile, statement history, score breakdowns, per-field provenance), GET /status (coverage, freshness, rate budget, provider health), GET /healthz. Serves the built SPA statically at /. No auth by design: single self-hosted operator (ADR-0002), bound to localhost/compose network by default.

acceptance.given: the API is running with a snapshot
acceptance.then: it returns 200 with rows, total count, page info and tookMs, with p95 latency under 500 ms warm for full-universe screens

acceptance.given: a DSL error or an unknown company symbol
acceptance.then: the API returns 422 (DSL error: message, position, hint) or 404 (unknown symbol) — never a 5xx — and logs the request with its outcome

acceptance.given: the SPA build exists
acceptance.then: the built SPA is served (index.html + hashed assets) and every /api route remains reachable under the same origin

functional.title: React/Vite SPA
functional.description: The React 18 + Vite + TypeScript SPA [E18][E21]: a dense, dark-first results grid on TanStack Table [E19] — query bar bound to the DSL, sortable columns, column picker, presets menu, CSV export of the current result set, and a company-detail drawer (statements, score breakdowns, provenance and freshness badges). The table is the hero; screener.in-class information density is the benchmark [E75].

acceptance.given: the SPA is served and a snapshot exists
acceptance.when: the user runs a DSL query from the query bar
acceptance.then: the grid renders the matching rows with sortable columns and a column picker, updates in under 1 second p95 for full-universe screens, and CSV export downloads exactly the rows and columns currently displayed

acceptance.given: the API is unreachable or returns a DSL error
acceptance.then: the UI surfaces the API's error message and hint inline (no blank screen, no console-only failure) and keeps the previous results visible

acceptance.given: a user opens a company row
acceptance.then: it shows the statement history, each score with its component breakdown and per-field provenance badges without navigating away from the results

functional.title: Docker Compose deployment
functional.description: Docker Compose deployment [E94]: service ingest (continuous crawler) + service api (FastAPI serving the built SPA), a shared volume for Parquet/DuckDB, healthchecks on both, .env consumed for optional phase-2 keys. docker compose up with zero keys yields a fully working system — the zero-key guarantee is exercised in CI.

acceptance.given: a machine with only Docker installed and no API keys in the environment
acceptance.then: both services report healthy within 120 seconds, the shared volume holds the DuckDB database and Parquet layers, and a screen executed through the UI or CLI returns rows once the bootstrap sample is ingested

acceptance.given: the ingest container crashes or is killed
acceptance.then: crawling resumes from the persisted queue without re-fetching fresh symbols, and the api service keeps serving the last snapshot uninterrupted throughout

acceptance.given: an operator provides phase-2 keys via .env
acceptance.then: the corresponding plugins activate without any image rebuild, and removing the keys returns the system to keyless operation

functional.title: Preset screens
functional.description: Preset screens shipped as plain, visible, editable DSL strings — transparency is the product (the closed ranks of Stockopedia [E67] are the counter-model): piotroski-strong (piotroski >= 7 [E39]), altman-safe (altman_z > 2.99), beneish-red-flags (beneish_m > -1.78), classic-value (low EV/EBIT + high ROIC), quality (high ROE + low leverage). Available identically via CLI (--preset), API (GET /presets) and the SPA presets menu.

acceptance.given: the presets are shipped
acceptance.when: GET /presets is called or crible screen --preset piotroski-strong runs
acceptance.then: each preset exposes its name, a one-line description and its complete DSL string, and running the preset is byte-for-byte equivalent to running that DSL string directly

acceptance.given: a user edits a preset's DSL in the UI
acceptance.then: it executes as ordinary DSL (presets carry no hidden logic) and the edited text can be saved as a new named preset

functional.title: ESEF XBRL Europe enrichment
functional.description: Europe-depth enrichment from filings.xbrl.org — the free, keyless ESEF repository (4,000+ audited annual reports as xBRL-JSON, with a JSON-API; explicitly the interim source until ESAP opens in July 2027 [E5][E15][E74]). Audited annual figures are stored as provider='esef' facts; where an audited value and a scraped Yahoo value coexist for the same field/period, the audited value wins and material discrepancies are logged. Company detail links to the underlying filing.

acceptance.given: an EU company whose ESEF annual report exists on filings.xbrl.org
acceptance.then: audited annual figures parsed from xBRL-JSON are stored as provider='esef' facts for that company, the snapshot marks the enriched fields' provenance as audited, and the company detail view links to the filing URL

acceptance.given: filings.xbrl.org is unreachable
acceptance.then: yfinance-derived data remains intact, the cycle records the outage and resumes at the next cycle — no partial overwrite of existing facts

acceptance.given: an audited ESEF value and a Yahoo value differ by more than 5% for the same field and period
acceptance.then: the audited value is used in the snapshot and the discrepancy is logged with both values, the field, the period and the filing reference

functional.title: Stooq price fallback
functional.description: Stooq CSV fallback for prices: when Yahoo throttles or hangs (a real, recurring failure mode [E36][E82]), the price refresher falls back to Stooq's keyless CSV endpoints (~21k global tickers, prices only) so valuation ratios (P/E, EV multiples) stay refreshable during Yahoo outages. Fetched bars carry provider='stooq' provenance.

acceptance.given: Yahoo price requests are being rate-limited or failing
acceptance.when: the price refresher runs its fallback path
acceptance.then: OHLCV bars for Stooq-covered tickers are updated from Stooq with provider='stooq' provenance and the dependent valuation ratios recompute on the next compute cycle

acceptance.given: a ticker unknown to Stooq
acceptance.then: the ticker is skipped with a single log line and remains scheduled for Yahoo on the next cycle — the fallback never blocks the queue

functional.title: Company detail view
functional.description: Company detail (SPA drawer + GET /company/{symbol}): full statement history as far as sources allow, every score with its complete component breakdown (the 9 Piotroski criteria pass/fail, the 8 Beneish components, Altman inputs), per-field provenance (provider + fetchedAt) and freshness. The transparency answer to Simply Wall St's polished-but-closed company pages [E58].

acceptance.given: a company present in the snapshot
acceptance.when: its detail is opened in the UI or fetched via the API
acceptance.then: it shows the held statement history, each score with its full component breakdown (9 Piotroski criteria, 8 Beneish components, Altman inputs), and per-field provenance with provider and fetch timestamp

acceptance.given: a company in the universe that has not been crawled yet
acceptance.then: the view shows the universe metadata (name, country, sector, exchange) plus its queue position / crawl ETA instead of an error

functional.title: Phase-2 free-key provider plugins
functional.description: Phase-2 free-key provider plugins behind the same Provider interface: financialreports (FinancialReports.eu — free official MCP server, OAuth, EU filings + normalized financials [E72][E73]), simfin (free bulk US fundamentals, ~12-month delay [E65][E66][E78]), fmp_free and eodhd_free (schema validation against the future paid switch only). Strictly optional: without its key a plugin logs one 'disabled (no key)' line and the system behaves exactly as keyless.

acceptance.given: no provider keys are configured
acceptance.then: each keyed plugin logs exactly one 'disabled (no key configured)' line, is reported as disabled in crible status, and every keyless flow behaves identically to a build without the plugins

acceptance.given: a valid SimFin key is configured
acceptance.then: SimFin bulk US fundamentals are stored as provider='simfin' raw facts alongside yfinance data without overwriting fresher facts, and provider health for simfin appears in crible status

acceptance.given: a configured key is invalid or expired
acceptance.when: the plugin makes its first API call
acceptance.then: the plugin disables itself for the session with a clear log line naming the env var to fix, and the crawler continues keyless without error

functional.title: EODHD paid provider PRD + stub plugin
functional.description: The single planned paid upgrade, specced without paying: docs/prds/eodhd.md — a detailed PRD for EODHD's Fundamentals Data Feed (€59.99/mo, 100k calls/day, worldwide fundamentals; free tier confirmed to exclude fundamentals [E61][E62]) with endpoint schemas validated against the demo tickers using the existing free key, field mapping to crible's raw schema, and the switch plan (set EODHD_KEY → plugin activates). docs/prds/fmp-ultimate.md documents FMP Ultimate ($149/mo) as the evaluated-and-rejected alternative.

acceptance.given: the repository is checked out
acceptance.then: it contains the pricing and quota facts (€59.99/mo, 100k calls/day), recorded sample payloads for the fundamentals and EOD endpoints captured via the free key's demo tickers, a field-by-field mapping to crible's raw schema, and the exact activation steps — and docs/prds/fmp-ultimate.md documents the rejected FMP alternative with its pricing

acceptance.given: an EODHD key is configured
acceptance.then: it validates the key with a single metadata call and reports the detected tier — a free-tier key yields 'insufficient tier for fundamentals' and the plugin stays disabled — proving the switch path end-to-end without a paid subscription

nonFunctional.statement: Screening is interactive: any valid DSL screen over the full snapshot answers in about a second on ordinary hardware — the DuckDB-over-Parquet engine, not a job queue [E4][E12].
nonFunctional.metric: p95 < 1 s end-to-end for any DSL screen over the full ~161k-row × ~200-column snapshot on a 2020-class laptop; API POST /screen p95 < 500 ms warm; GET /company/{symbol} p95 < 300 ms.

nonFunctional.statement: No auth by design (single self-hosted operator, ADR-0002); the attack surface is the DSL and the provider keys. DSL input can never become SQL; keys never leave the environment.
nonFunctional.metric: DSL compiles to parametrized SQL over a strict column whitelist (property-tested, see NFR-011); API binds to localhost/compose network by default; provider keys read only from environment/.env — never baked into images, logged, or echoed in errors; Python and npm dependencies audited in CI.

nonFunctional.statement: The system degrades gracefully and recovers from transient failures without data loss: the crawler survives kills and rate limits; readers never see partial data.
nonFunctional.metric: Crash-resume without duplicate fetches inside freshness windows; snapshot publication is atomic (write-new-then-swap; a reader never observes a half-written snapshot); raw Parquet writes are write-temp-then-rename; a 24 h crawler soak run completes with zero unhandled exceptions.

nonFunctional.statement: An operator reaches a first successful screen without reading source code; every error names the next action.
nonFunctional.metric: Clean machine → first successful screen in ≤ 3 commands (docker compose up; wait for bootstrap sample; run a preset); every CLI/API/UI error message names the failing input or the command to run next; presets give one-click value on first contact.

nonFunctional.statement: The operator can answer 'what is the crawler doing, how fresh is my data, am I inside the rate budget?' without reading code.
nonFunctional.metric: Structured JSON log line for every provider fetch (symbol, provider, latency, outcome); crible status and GET /status expose universe count, fundamentals coverage %, freshness histogram, rolling req/h and per-provider health; per-symbol consecutive-failure counts are queryable.

nonFunctional.statement: Data cost is €0 in v1 and can only ever grow by one explicit, documented switch (EODHD).
nonFunctional.metric: Zero paid dependencies in v1 (keyless sources only [E61]); the only planned recurring cost is EODHD Fundamentals €59.99/mo behind the EODHD_KEY switch documented in docs/prds/eodhd.md; infra = any single Docker host.

nonFunctional.statement: The crawler never exceeds Yahoo's tolerated request rate — staying welcome is a hard constraint of the keyless design, not an optimisation [E3].
nonFunctional.metric: Rolling 60-minute Yahoo request count ≤ 330 (≈10% headroom under the ~360/h tolerance [E3]), enforced by a token bucket and asserted in tests under simulated load; 0 budget violations in a 24 h soak; on HTTP 429 the backoff doubles from 1 min to a 15 min cap with ±20% jitter [E52].

nonFunctional.statement: The filter engine keeps its millisecond-class promise as the snapshot grows.
nonFunctional.metric: CI runs the DSL engine against a synthetic full-size snapshot (161k rows × 200 columns): every preset and a battery of generated screens must complete with p95 < 1 s; the DuckDB query plan for any DSL screen touches only the snapshot (no row-by-row Python loop).

nonFunctional.statement: Zero-key operation is a guaranteed, permanent contract — not a degraded mode. Everything core runs from one docker compose up with no accounts and no external services.
nonFunctional.metric: A dedicated CI job runs the full E2E suite with an empty environment (no provider keys) and must pass for every release; docker compose up on a clean machine requires nothing but Docker; removing every key from a configured install returns it to a fully working state.

nonFunctional.statement: Every number can explain itself: formula, source and fetch time. Audited beats scraped. This is the product's moat against closed SaaS ranks [E67].
nonFunctional.metric: Every snapshot cell is traceable to (provider, fetchedAt) and a published formula (financetoolkit's open implementations [E1] or crible's own tested code); Piotroski/Altman/Beneish are unit-tested against published examples; ESEF audited values override scraped values, with >5% discrepancies logged (FR-010).

nonFunctional.statement: No DSL input, however hostile, can execute unintended SQL.
nonFunctional.metric: Property-based tests feed ≥ 1,000 generated/mutated inputs per run: any input that parses must compile to SQL referencing only whitelisted columns with every literal bound as a parameter; any input that does not parse must execute nothing. A curated corpus of injection payloads is asserted rejected.

nonFunctional.statement: Components stay isolated (universe / ingest / compute / store / api / ui) and the provider seam is the extension point — adding a source never touches the core.
nonFunctional.metric: Adding a data provider = one plugin module implementing the Provider interface + its tests, zero changes to scheduler/compute/API; every FR has at least one test whose name carries the FR id (verified by construct verify --strict); TDD red-green-refactor throughout.

nonFunctional.statement: Fully self-hosted: the instance calls data sources, and nothing else. No telemetry, ever.
nonFunctional.metric: Zero outbound requests except to configured data-source endpoints (asserted by an egress test using a recording proxy in CI); no analytics/telemetry libraries in either lockfile; all data lives under the operator's data directory/volume.

architecture.context: crible serves a fundamental investor and self-hosting tinkerer screening European + worldwide equities. Pipeline: universe (FinanceDatabase CSVs → DuckDB) → ingest (rolling prioritized keyless crawler over yfinance + ESEF XBRL + Stooq behind a Provider plugin seam, raw versioned Parquet) → compute (financetoolkit ratios + Piotroski/Altman + in-house Beneish → wide snapshot Parquet) → store/query (DuckDB over Parquet; DSL → parametrized SQL) → surfaces (Typer CLI, FastAPI HTTP API, React/Vite SPA) — all packaged as two Docker Compose services (ingest, api) sharing one data volume. No external services are required in zero-key mode; each external data source is isolated behind the Provider Plugin API (ADR-0003, ADR-0004).

attributes.name: region
attributes.type: string (europe | us | world — drives crawl priority)

attributes.name: priority
attributes.type: int (0 = europe, 1 = us large caps, 2 = world)

attributes.name: id
attributes.type: identifier (yfinance | esef | stooq | simfin | financialreports | fmp_free | eodhd_free | eodhd)

attributes.name: enabled
attributes.type: boolean (derived: keyless → true; keyed → key present & valid)

attributes.name: health
attributes.type: json (last success, error counts, budget usage)

interfaces.name: CLI
interfaces.summary: The crible command (Typer): screen (DSL → table/CSV), ingest (bootstrap/once/loop), compute, status, export. Exit codes and stderr messages are part of the contract; same DSL semantics as the API.

interfaces.name: HTTP API
interfaces.summary: FastAPI: POST /screen, GET /screen.csv, GET /presets, GET /company/{symbol}, GET /status, GET /healthz; serves the built SPA at /. JSON errors carry message + hint; 422 for DSL errors, 404 for unknown symbols, never 5xx for user input.

interfaces.name: Web App
interfaces.summary: React/Vite SPA: query bar (DSL), TanStack results grid (sort, column picker, CSV export), presets menu, company-detail drawer, status view. Talks only to the HTTP API on the same origin.

interfaces.name: Provider Plugin API
interfaces.summary: Internal contract every data source implements: capabilities() (statements/prices/coverage), fetch_statements(symbol), fetch_prices(symbols), health(). Keyless providers are always on; keyed providers self-disable without their env key (one log line, no crash). External endpoints behind it: Yahoo via yfinance, filings.xbrl.org JSON-API, Stooq CSV; phase 2: FinancialReports.eu, SimFin, FMP free, EODHD free; future: EODHD paid.

adrs.title: Primary technology stack
adrs.context: Solo developer, TDD, zero-ops self-hosting, and a data problem shaped like: a 161k-symbol universe [E2], per-ticker scraping through a fragile rate-limited source [E3], columnar analytics over ~200 ratio columns, and a dense data UI. The stack must minimise moving parts while keeping every layer replaceable.
adrs.decision: Python 3.12 for universe/ingest/compute/API (the finance OSS ecosystem is Python: financetoolkit [E1][E8], financedatabase [E2], yfinance [E3][E98]); DuckDB over Parquet (pyarrow) as the only datastore — embedded, zero-ops, columnar, millisecond screens [E4][E12][E10]; FastAPI [E13] + Typer [E92] as the two entry points sharing one core; React 18 + Vite + TypeScript with TanStack Table for the SPA [E18][E19][E21]; pytest for TDD [E95]; Docker Compose for deployment [E94].
adrs.consequences: One language for the whole data path; no database server, migrations or queue to operate; the SPA is the only build step. DuckDB is single-writer — the ingest and API processes share data via Parquet files and an atomic snapshot swap rather than concurrent writes. Python-side compute must stay vectorised (pandas/DuckDB) to hold the performance NFRs.
adrs.alternatives: OpenBB Platform as an aggregation façade — rejected: heavy dependency whose free path is still yfinance underneath, and its screener providers cannot screen Europe [E90][E91]. Postgres + Celery + React (the xang1234/stock-screener architecture) — rejected: real prior art shows preset-filter timeouts and index churn at exactly this workload, i.e. an operational database doing an analytical job [E77][E88]. A Rust custom engine — rejected: re-implements what DuckDB already does in-process.

adrs.title: Self-hosting, zero-key contract and data ownership
adrs.context: Every credible fundamental screener with European coverage is subscription SaaS with closed formulas (Stockopedia [E67], Simply Wall St [E58], Uncle Stock [E59]); data feeds worth using are paid (EODHD fundamentals start at €59.99/mo [E61]). The product's reason to exist is owning the screener: data, formulas, universe.
adrs.decision: Ship as a self-hosted Docker Compose deployment where the host owns all data. The zero-key mode (FinanceDatabase + yfinance + filings.xbrl.org + Stooq) is a permanent, CI-enforced contract — every core flow works with no account, no key, no external service. No auth layer: single operator, bound to the local network; no telemetry.
adrs.consequences: Data residency and Yahoo-tolerance compliance are the operator's responsibility (documented); the free path accepts Yahoo's shallow history (~4 annual periods [E98]) and ~19-day full-universe sweep as the price of €0; a hosted multi-tenant SaaS is explicitly out of scope.
adrs.alternatives: Hosted SaaS — rejected: recreates the incumbents' model and their privacy/lock-in problems [E67][E58]. Mandatory free-tier keys (FMP/EODHD free) in v1 — rejected: their free tiers contribute nothing for Europe (US-only / no fundamentals [E61]) and would break the zero-key promise.

adrs.title: Persistence: DuckDB over layered Parquet with atomic snapshot swap
adrs.context: Three data shapes coexist: append-heavy raw fetches (per provider, re-parseable), a wide computed snapshot the screener hammers, and small operational state (crawl queue, provider health). Readers (API/CLI) and the writer (ingest/compute) are separate processes [E4].
adrs.decision: A layered store on one shared volume: (1) raw layer — immutable, versioned Parquet per provider/fetch (write-temp-then-rename), the durable source of truth that survives any recompute; (2) snapshot layer — the wide company × period Parquet, published by atomic swap (write new, fsync, rename; readers re-open on change); (3) operational state — a small DuckDB database owned by the ingest process. The API opens Parquet read-only through DuckDB [E12][E10]; every external integration writes only through its provider adapter into the raw layer.
adrs.consequences: No write contention (single-writer per layer); any snapshot is reproducible from the raw layer; corruption recovery = delete snapshot, recompute. Cross-layer consistency is eventual (a screen can lag a fetch by one compute cycle) — acceptable for quarterly-moving fundamentals and surfaced via freshness metadata.
adrs.alternatives: One shared DuckDB database for everything — rejected: concurrent writer/reader processes across containers fight DuckDB's single-writer model. Postgres — rejected: an operational row store doing an analytical job, plus a server to operate (see ADR-0001 and the xang1234 timeout lesson [E88]). Event sourcing — deferred: the raw Parquet layer already gives replayability without the machinery.

adrs.title: Keyless ingestion: rolling prioritized crawl under a hard Yahoo rate budget
adrs.context: Yahoo has no bulk fundamentals endpoint — statements are per-ticker calls — and tolerates roughly 360 req/h before 429s; maintainers treat rate-limiting as Yahoo policy, wontfix [E3]. Users report failures from ~950 tickers per session and intermittent hangs [E36][E82]. yfinance now rides curl_cffi with impersonated sessions and must own its session (no requests-cache injection) [E52][E99]. At ~330 req/h a full 161k-symbol fundamentals sweep takes ≈ 19 days — but fundamentals move quarterly, so a rolling crawl is sufficient by design.
adrs.decision: A continuous priority-queue crawler with a global token bucket (≤ 330 req/h, 10% headroom), jittered exponential backoff on 429 (1 min → 15 min cap), a per-request watchdog against hangs, persisted queue state for crash-resume, and freshness-driven revisits (quarterly statements, daily prices). Priority tiers: Europe → US large caps → rest of world. Europe's cross-sectional gaps are compensated by the audited ESEF layer (FR-010); prices fail over to Stooq (FR-011).
adrs.consequences: Full worldwide coverage builds progressively over ~3 weeks from first boot (Europe first, visible in crible status); the crawler must be a long-lived, stateful, polite process rather than a batch job; the single-switch EODHD upgrade (FR-014) replaces exactly this fragile link if the operator ever pays.
adrs.alternatives: Parallel scraping with proxy rotation — rejected: hostile to Yahoo's tolerance, fragile, and against the polite-by-design constraint [E3]. Paid feed from day one — rejected: breaks the €0 v1 budget and the zero-key contract (ADR-0002). On-demand fetching only (no crawl) — rejected: screening needs the full cross-section precomputed; per-request fetching cannot fill a 161k universe interactively.

competitors.name: Finviz
competitors.note: The reference free screener — US-only. Rich presets and thematic filters, but no European exchange coverage at all; Europe is exactly the gap crible fills.

competitors.name: TradingView Stock Screener
competitors.note: Broad multi-market SaaS screener; fundamental depth and export sit behind subscription tiers, formulas are opaque, and nothing is self-hostable.

competitors.name: Simply Wall St
competitors.note: Polished fundamental-analysis SaaS with genuinely global coverage (praised for value-investing breadth) — but closed data, closed methodology, subscription-gated. crible's company detail is the transparent answer to its snowflake pages.

competitors.name: Stockopedia
competitors.note: Quality/Value/Momentum ranks over European + global markets, subscription SaaS. Its StockRanks are precisely the kind of closed composite score crible replaces with visible, editable DSL presets.

competitors.name: Uncle Stock
competitors.note: 'Professional stock screening for DIY investors' — the closest functional cousin for European fundamental screening, with backtesting; subscription, closed, not self-hostable.

competitors.name: screener.in
competitors.note: India's fundamental screener — the UX benchmark: information-dense tables, transparent query language, fast. crible aims for this feel over a worldwide, Europe-deep universe.

competitors.name: EODHD Screener API
competitors.note: The data vendor's own screener endpoint. Fundamentals require the €59.99/mo tier (free tier: 20 calls/day, 1-year EOD, no fundamentals) — this is the single paid upgrade crible keeps on standby (FR-014), not a v1 dependency.

competitors.name: Find My Moat
competitors.note: EU-filings-based research tool built on the FinancialReports.eu data — evidence that the European-filings angle carries a product, and a pointer to the free MCP server crible integrates in phase 2 (FR-013).

competitors.name: OpenBB Platform
competitors.note: Open-source multi-provider financial data platform (70k★). Considered as a foundation and rejected (ADR-0001): its free equity-fundamentals path is still yfinance underneath, and its screener providers (finviz et al.) cannot screen Europe.

oss.name: JerBouma/FinanceToolkit
oss.note: Core dependency: 200+ transparently-implemented ratios + Piotroski & Altman (Beneish absent — crible implements it). Yahoo backend supported via enforce_source; built for portfolios, so crible owns batching/backoff.

oss.name: JerBouma/FinanceDatabase
oss.note: The universe: 160,995 equities across 117 countries as plain CSVs, symbols Yahoo-suffixed. Its issue tracker documents the data-quality caveats crible tolerates (CUSIP collisions, exchange-code drift) and the steady quality PRs that fix them.

oss.name: ranaroussi/yfinance
oss.note: The fragile-but-free fundamentals link: curl_cffi sessions, ~360 req/h tolerance, per-ticker statements ~4y deep, hangs and 429s documented in issues — ADR-0004 designs around every one of these.

oss.name: xang1234/stock-screener
oss.note: Closest architectural prior art (FastAPI + Postgres + Celery + React, multi-exchange refresh queues). Its preset-filtering timeout fix is the cautionary tale that justified DuckDB-over-Parquet instead of an operational DB.

oss.name: astro30/valinvest
oss.note: Clean reference implementation of Piotroski-style fundamental scoring (dead since 2023, FMP-bound) — used as a cross-check for crible's score implementations, not as a dependency.

oss.name: SimFin/simfin
oss.note: Official Python client for SimFin's free bulk US fundamentals (~12-month delay). Library dormant since 2023 → crible's phase-2 simfin plugin calls the REST API directly.

buildPlan.title: M1 — Walking skeleton (must-haves)
buildPlan.outcome: A usable end-to-end slice covering every must-have requirement.

buildPlan.title: M2 — Rounded product (should-haves)
buildPlan.outcome: The product is complete enough for real users.

buildPlan.title: M3 — Enhancements (could-haves)
buildPlan.outcome: Nice-to-have capabilities that differentiate the product.

principles: The table is the hero — information density over decoration; every pixel of chrome must earn its place against one more visible row.
Numbers first: tabular figures, right-aligned, monospaced; color encodes meaning (gain/loss/flag), never mood.
Dark mode is the primary theme; light is the variant. Both ship, both meet contrast.
Transparency in the UI itself: every score links to its components, every value to its provenance — no unexplained numbers.
Consistency over novelty — reuse tokens and components before inventing new ones.
Accessible by construction: dense ≠ inaccessible; every flow works with keyboard and assistive tech.

tokens.name: color.primary
tokens.note: Actions, links, focused query bar. Light: #0969DA. AA on both bgs.

tokens.name: color.warn
tokens.note: Stale-data badges, Beneish red flags. Light: #9A6700.

tokens.name: font.mono
tokens.note: All numerals (tabular-nums), DSL query bar, code.

components.name: App Shell & Navigation
components.purpose: Slim top bar (product name, status pill with coverage/freshness, presets menu, theme toggle) framing the full-height screener.

components.name: Query Bar (DSL editor)
components.purpose: Monospaced input for the DSL with syntax-aware validation, inline error (token + position + hint), field autocomplete from the whitelist, and run-on-Enter.

components.name: Results Grid
components.purpose: TanStack Table: virtualised rows, sortable columns, sticky header, tabular-numeral cells with gain/loss coloring, row click → detail drawer.

components.name: Column Picker
components.purpose: Searchable multi-select over the ~200 snapshot columns, grouped by family (valuation, profitability, solvency, scores…); persists locally.

components.name: Presets Menu
components.purpose: Named screens with description and the full DSL visible; one click loads the DSL into the query bar for editing (never hidden logic).

components.name: Company Detail Drawer
components.purpose: Right-side drawer: profile header, statement history table, score cards with component breakdowns (9 Piotroski / 8 Beneish / Altman inputs), provenance + freshness badges, ESEF filing link.

components.name: Status Dashboard
components.purpose: Coverage %, freshness histogram, rolling req/h vs budget, per-provider health — the operator's window into the rolling crawl.

components.name: Export Button
components.purpose: Downloads the current result set (rows + visible columns) as CSV via GET /screen.csv; disabled with reason when no results.

components.name: Feedback & Notifications
components.purpose: Inline banners and toasts for API errors (with hint), long-running states and export completion; polite live regions.

components.name: Empty & Error States
components.purpose: First-run (universe loading, crawl progress with ETA), no-match (suggest loosening clauses), API-down and not-yet-crawled company states — each teaches the next action.

screens.name: Screener
screens.purpose: The home screen: query bar + presets + results grid + export. Where 95% of time is spent; everything else is a drawer or a pill away.

screens.name: Company detail
screens.purpose: Drawer (deep-linkable route) over the screener: statements, score breakdowns, provenance, ESEF filing link.

screens.name: Ingest & coverage status
screens.purpose: The crawl observatory: universe coverage, freshness histogram, rate-budget gauge, provider health, recent failures.

screens.name: Providers & settings
screens.purpose: Read-only provider inventory (keyless / keyed-off / keyed-on with health), pointer to .env configuration and the EODHD upgrade path; theme preference.

flows.name: First run (zero-key)
steps: docker compose up with no keys
Status shows universe bootstrap then Europe-first crawl progress with ETA
First preset screen returns rows on the ingested sample
Grid renders; the operator saves a first custom DSL screen

flows.name: Screen & export
steps: Type or edit DSL in the query bar (autocomplete from whitelist)
Run: results in < 1 s, sortable, column picker adjusts the view
Export CSV of exactly what is displayed

flows.name: Investigate a company
steps: Click a result row — the detail drawer opens without losing the result set
Read score component breakdowns and per-field provenance
Follow the ESEF filing link for the audited source

flows.name: Enable a phase-2 provider
steps: Add the provider key to .env and restart the stack
Provider flips to enabled in the status view with live health
New facts appear with their provider provenance

flows.name: Watch the rolling crawl
steps: Check coverage %, freshness histogram and req/h vs budget
Drill into recent failures and parked symbols

requirements.statement: Every interactive control is fully keyboard operable.

acceptance.given: a user navigates with the keyboard only
acceptance.then: every interactive control is reachable, operable and follows a logical focus order

acceptance.given: an element receives keyboard focus
acceptance.then: a visible focus indicator is shown and meets the non-text contrast minimum

requirements.statement: Colour contrast meets the target standard.

acceptance.given: any text or essential UI element
acceptance.when: it is rendered in any supported theme
acceptance.then: contrast meets the target (≥ 4.5:1 for body text, ≥ 3:1 for large text and UI)

requirements.statement: Every control and image exposes an accessible name.

acceptance.given: a form control, icon-only button or meaningful image
acceptance.when: it is read by assistive technology
acceptance.then: it exposes a programmatic label/name and images carry meaningful alt text (decorative images are hidden)

requirements.statement: Structure and async changes are conveyed semantically.

acceptance.given: a screen is parsed by a screen reader
acceptance.then: headings, landmarks and roles convey the structure and live regions announce asynchronous changes

requirements.statement: Reduced motion and zoom are respected.

acceptance.given: a user prefers reduced motion or zooms to 200%
acceptance.then: non-essential motion is reduced or disabled and content reflows without loss of content or function

contentVoice: Voice & tone: sober, precise, numbers-forward; French product name, English UI.
Label actions with the outcome the user gets, not the system operation behind it.
Error messages state what happened, why, and the next step — never blame the user.
Empty states teach the first useful action; success states confirm exactly what changed.
